params.text:
Turning complaints into better care

A complaint is only truly resolved when the service learns from it. Responding to the individual matters, but the real value comes from asking, “What does this tell us, and how do we stop it happening again?” This is exactly what CQC Regulation 16 means by acting on complaints.

  • Look for themes and patterns across complaints, not just one-off incidents.
  • Feed the learning into training, procedures and staffing so real change happens.
  • Close the loop by telling the person, where appropriate, what changed as a result of their complaint — this shows their voice made a difference.
  • Share learning with the team so the whole service improves, not just one worker.

The link to duty of candour

The duty of candour (CQC Regulation 20) requires services to be open and honest when something goes wrong, especially when a person has been harmed. That means telling the person what happened, apologising, and explaining what will be done to put it right — whether or not they have complained. Candour and good complaints handling go hand in hand: both are built on honesty, openness and a genuine willingness to learn.

params.question: What best shows that a service is genuinely learning from complaints?

answers.text: It looks for patterns and changes its training, procedures or staffing as a result.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Acting on themes and making real changes is what learning from complaints looks like.

answers.text: It records the complaint but changes nothing afterwards.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Recording alone is not enough; the point is to act and improve.

answers.text: It treats every complaint as a one-off with no wider lessons.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Ignoring patterns means the same problems keep recurring.

answers.text: It discourages people from complaining so the numbers look good.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Suppressing complaints hides problems and puts people at risk — the opposite of learning.
